steps.title: Identify the target population vs. the sample
steps.content: The council wanted to assess opinions of all city residents, but only surveyed residents who own dogs. These are not the same group. All non-dog-owning residents were excluded from the sample.

steps.title: Identify why this creates bias
steps.content: Dog owners have a direct personal interest in a dog park. Their preferences systematically differ from those of residents who do not own dogs. A sample drawn entirely from one interested subgroup cannot represent the full population.

steps.title: Evaluate all choices
steps.content:
• A: We cannot conclude the majority of all residents favor the park based on a biased sample. The sample does not represent all residents. Eliminated.
  • B: More dog owners would not fix the bias — it would make it worse by further over-representing one group. Eliminated.
  • C: Surveying only non-dog-owners would create the opposite bias. The goal is a representative sample, not one skewed the other way. Eliminated.
  • D: The sample is biased because it draws exclusively from dog owners, who are not representative of all city residents. That confirms D is correct.
